items.mistake: Using `set(list)` to dedupe when order matters.
items.fix: Use an order-preserving dedupe with a `seen` set or `dict.fromkeys`.
items.why: Sets are unordered; you lose the original sequence, which often matters for ranked retrieval results.

items.mistake: Trying to store a list (or dict, or set) inside a set.
items.fix: Convert to a hashable type — a tuple of hashables, or a `frozenset`.
items.why: Set elements must be hashable; mutables can't be hashed safely.
items.example:
```python
seen = set()
seen.add(tuple(path))        # OK — tuple is hashable
seen.add(frozenset(labels))  # OK — frozenset is hashable
```

items.mistake: Calling `set.remove(x)` when x might not be present.
items.why: `remove` raises `KeyError`; `discard` is a no-op for missing elements.
items.example:
```python
seen.discard(item_id)   # safe even if item_id wasn't there
```

items.mistake: Relying on a set's iteration order in logs, prompts or tests.
items.fix: Sort it (`sorted(s)`) or use a list/dict if order is part of the contract.
items.why: CPython's set iteration is implementation-defined and can change between versions or runs.

items.fix: `{}` is an empty *dict*; use `set()` for an empty set.
items.why: `{}` was assigned to dicts first in Python's history; the literal syntax for sets needs at least one element.
